title: Professional Trolls on YouTube of the future when TV dies

enrichment.summary: Kyle speculates about what happens when TV viewership declines and people migrate to YouTube, predicting that wealthy interests currently funding Fox News will instead distribute money across thousands of YouTube channels as 'professional trolls' to maintain ideological influence. He connects this to observations about the relationship between wealthy elites and the religious Republican base, arguing the rich don't actually share the beliefs they fund but use religion as a control mechanism. He ties this speculation to a personal anecdote about a YouTuber named George Potato who demanded payment before promoting anything, which Kyle sees as evidence of paid YouTube influence operations.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: TV is dying and wealthy interests will redirect media money to YouTube channels
Believes funded troll networks on YouTube are likely or already exist
Rich conservatives don't actually believe in the religion they fund — they use it to control the base
The poorest and least educated Americans are most religious; religion is an easy out for people who 'fuck up'
References them as a real precedent for the kind of funded influence he's describing

entities: Kim Davis (implied — county clerk who denied gay marriage licenses, married four times)

claims.subject: status quo
claims.claim: The status quo for power and the power structure is trying to maintain itself when Bernie Sanders gets elected.

claims.subject: people
claims.claim: People stop looking at themselves when they stop watching TV and start watching YouTube.

claims.subject: YouTube
claims.claim: YouTube videos are starting to show pharmaceutical ads and ads for television shows.

claims.subject: news
claims.claim: When people stop watching news and focus on YouTube, it becomes popular.

claims.subject: websites
claims.claim: People start thinking about how websites might try anything funky and weird to keep people engaged.

claims.subject: professional trolls
claims.claim: There might be professional trolls funded by big money to push certain narratives on YouTube.

claims.subject: Fox News
claims.claim: Fox News could be broken up and replaced by a thousand different YouTube channels funded by big money.

claims.subject: Republican base
claims.claim: The religious base of the Republican Party is not necessarily aligned with the beliefs of the rich elite.

claims.subject: religion
claims.claim: Religion is an easy way out for people who are dumb and mess up all the time.

claims.subject: Christianity
claims.claim: Christianity has evolved into a religion where people can just say they love Jesus and forget about their bad actions.

claims.subject: rich people
claims.claim: Rich people might not actually believe in Jesus but use religion to control the base.

claims.subject: YouTube channels
claims.claim: There could be enclaves of people on YouTube who are paid trolls pushing certain agendas.

claims.subject: George Potato
claims.claim: George Potato is a YouTube guy who got busted for doing stuff just for money.

claims.subject: YouTube culture
claims.claim: There are small cultures on YouTube with only a dozen people or less talking about niche topics.

claims.subject: Russians
claims.claim: The Russians had a $15 million yearly budget for trolls.

claims.subject: Fox News
claims.claim: Fox News might disintegrate as people stop watching TV and move to YouTube.

claims.subject: YouTube channels
claims.claim: Big money could fund fake YouTube channels to push certain narratives.

claims.subject: Donald Trump
claims.claim: Some people on YouTube taunt others by saying they love Donald Trump even when they don't.

claims.subject: professional trolls
claims.claim: Professional trolls might be behind some of the negative comments on YouTube.

claims.subject: YouTube
claims.claim: YouTube could become a forest of fake stories and funded channels pushing certain agendas.
